messages.content: Currently, a biofilm is defined as complex, three-dimensional communities of microorganisms that adhere to biotic and abiotic surfaces and are enclosed in a self-generated matrix of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS). The biofilm consists of water, microbial cells, EPS including polysaccharide substances, lipids, proteins, metal ions, nucleic acids and extracellular enzymes (Hrynyshyn et al, 2022; Tango et al, 2018; Verderosa et al, 2019; Percival et al, 2017; Guerra et al, 2022). - rephrase this

The current definition of a biofilm describes it as a complex community of microorganisms that attach to both living and non-living surfaces and are enclosed in a matrix of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS). This biofilm matrix consists of various components such as water, microbial cells, EPS made up of polysaccharide substances, lipids, proteins, metal ions, nucleic acids, and extracellular enzymes (Hrynyshyn et al, 2022; Tango et al, 2018; Verderosa et al, 2019; Percival et al, 2017; Guerra et al, 2022).

The process of creating a bacterial biofilm is multi-stage. It depends on environmental factors, the species of microorganisms and the specificity of the colonized materials (Gupta et al., 2018; Nguyen et al., 2011; Hayrapetyan et al., 2016; Vuotto et al., 2017). - rephrase this

The formation of a bacterial biofilm is a multi-stage process that is influenced by environmental factors, the microbial species involved, and the characteristics of the surfaces being colonized (Gupta et al., 2018; Nguyen et al., 2011; Hayrapetyan et al., 2016; Vuotto et al., 2017).

Dispersion can occur as a result of internal biochemical changes in cells, lack of nutrients, or increased shear stress (Bridier et al., 2015). The physicochemical properties of the substrate as well as the structure of bacterial cells affect the production of bacterial biofilm. Bacteria can adhere to the surfaces of plastics, glass and metals. Some cellular organelles, e.g. flagella, curli and fmbrie, enable and accelerate the adhesion of bacterial cells to surfaces.

Dispersion in biofilms can be triggered by various factors, including internal biochemical changes in cells, nutrient deprivation, or increased shear stress (Bridier et al., 2015). The production and structure of bacterial biofilms are influenced by the physicochemical properties of the substrate and the characteristics of bacterial cells. Bacteria have the ability to adhere to various surfaces like plastics, glass, and metals. Certain cellular organelles, such as flagella, curli, and fimbriae, play a role in facilitating and enhancing the adhesion of bacterial cells to surfaces.
Mathematical models are also a useful tool in preventing microbiological safety risks. Forecasting in microbiology reduces labor and time consumption of laboratory tests. - rephrase this

Mathematical models are valuable tools in mitigating microbiological safety risks, as they aid in predicting and forecasting outcomes in microbiology. By utilizing these models, the labor and time required for conducting laboratory tests can be reduced.
Studies by Weems et al. (2015), Gad et al. (2021), Traore et al. (2010), and Gemba et al. (2022) isolated E. cloacae from breast milk. - rephrase this
E. cloacae has been isolated from breast milk in studies conducted by Weems et al. (2015), Gad et al. (2021), Traore et al. (2010), and Gemba et al. (2022).

A very common problem reported by Breast Milk Banks is the contamination of milk with Bacillus cereus bacteria. The milk in the bank is pasteurized (62.5 °C; 30 minutes) and the pasteurization process does not inactivate B. cereus spores, which germinate into vegetative forms during pasteurization (Jandová et al., 2021; Adjide et al., 2022; Billeauda, 2021; Strom et al., 2022; Cortmontagne et al., 2021). In own research, Bacillus spp bacteria isolated from milk showed the ability to produce biofilm (Gemba et. al, 2022). - rephrase this

Breast Milk Banks commonly face the issue of milk contamination with Bacillus cereus bacteria. The milk stored in these banks undergoes pasteurization at a temperature of 62.5 °C for 30 minutes. However, this pasteurization process does not effectively eliminate the spores of B. cereus, which can then germinate into vegetative forms during pasteurization (Jandová et al., 2021; Adjide et al., 2022; Billeauda, 2021; Strom et al., 2022; Cortmontagne et al., 2021). In our own research, we observed that Bacillus spp. bacteria isolated from milk demonstrated the ability to produce biofilms (Gemba et al., 2022).

Enteral feeding tubes are made of polyvinyl chloride. Polypropylene is used in the production of bottles for storing and feeding babies, stainless steel is an example of a low-adhesive surface widely used in food production.

Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is commonly used in the production of enteral feeding tubes, while polypropylene is utilized in the manufacturing of bottles for storing and feeding babies. Stainless steel, known for its low-adhesive properties, is widely employed as a surface in food production.

Overnight cultures of bacterial isolates were incubated in Brain Heart Infusion (BHI) broth (Neogen, Poland) at 37°C. The target inoculum concentration in the cultures was achieved by diluting the overnight culture to 10 -7 CFU/ml and obtaining the following bacterial cell densities: low, 1 x 103 CFU/ml (S); medium, 1 x 10 5 CFU/ml (M) and large, 1 x 10 7 CFU/ml (L). The density of the inoculum was checked by surface plating at dilutions in the range of 10-5-10-7. - rephrase this

To prepare the bacterial cultures, overnight isolates were placed in Brain Heart Infusion (BHI) broth (Neogen, Poland) and then incubated at 37°C. The desired inoculum concentration in the cultures was achieved by diluting the overnight culture to a concentration of 10^-7 CFU/ml. This resulted in three different bacterial cell densities: low (1 x 10^3 CFU/ml), medium (1 x 10^5 CFU/ml), and high (1 x 10^7 CFU/ml). The density of the inoculum was confirmed through surface plating at dilutions ranging from 10^-5 to 10^-7.
Biofilm-bound crystal violet was dissolved in 200 µl of 96% ethanol (Poch, Poland) for 10 min. - rephrase this
To dissolve the crystal violet bound to the biofilm, 200 µl of 96% ethanol (Poch, Poland) was used for a duration of 10 minutes.

For each set of variables describing the phenomenon of biofilm formation: time, inoculum density, temperature, min. 10 different response surface models, from which, based on the evaluation of function parameters and the quality of statistical parameters (R 2 , DFAdj R 2 , SEFs, MSE), the best one was selected for presentation, which was a compromise between confidence in the model and its statistical correctness. - rephrase this

To analyze the biofilm formation phenomenon, numerous variables such as time, inoculum density, and temperature were considered. At least 10 different response surface models were evaluated. The selection of the best model was based on assessing the function parameters and statistical indicators (including R^2, DFAdj R^2, SEFs, MSE), aiming to strike a balance between confidence in the model and its statistical validity.
The most biofilm was formed by the analyzed bacteria between 12-18 h of incubation. - rephrase this
The bacteria under investigation exhibited the highest levels of biofilm formation during the incubation period of 12-18 hours.

At 30 h of incubation, biofilm was produced only by Bacillus spp. (weak biofilm - densities M and L), while after 36 h of incubation, biofilm was produced by E. cloacae (weak biofilm - density L and moderate biofilm - density M) and Bacillus spp., which produced strong biofilm (densities M and L) and weak biofilm (density S) (Table 3). - rephrase this

After 30 hours of incubation, only Bacillus spp. demonstrated the ability to produce biofilm, albeit weakly, at medium (M) and high (L) cell densities. However, at the 36-hour mark, biofilm production was observed in both E. cloacae, with a weak biofilm at high (L) and a moderate biofilm at medium (M) densities, and in Bacillus spp., which displayed a strong biofilm at medium (M) and high (L) densities, as well as a weak biofilm at low (S) density (refer to Table 3 for details).

In the case of models with 4 and 5 parameters, all parameter estimates are statistically significantly different from zero at the significance level of p<0.05, which is also indicated by the high value of the F statistic. This means that the parameters have been estimated precisely enough to have trust and that the model variables: inoculum time and density have a significant impact on the dependent variable of biofilm formation. In the case of models with 6 parameters, the parameters e and f had no significant effect on the phenomenon of biofilm formation. Noteworthy are the low asymptotic mean errors of parameter estimation (Standard Error), which allows us to assume that the parameters have been estimated precisely enough to be trusted. - rephrase this

In the models comprising four and five parameters, all parameter estimates were found to be statistically significant at a significance level of p<0.05. This significance is further supported by the high values of the F statistic. It indicates that the parameters have been accurately estimated, instilling confidence in their reliability. Additionally, the independent variables of inoculum time and density were found to have a significant impact on the dependent variable of biofilm formation. However, in models with six parameters, it was observed that parameters e and f did not significantly influence the phenomenon of biofilm formation. It is worth noting the low asymptotic mean errors of parameter estimation (Standard Error), which suggests precise estimation and reinforces the trustworthiness of the parameters.

The surfaces selected for testing are important in the preparation, distribution and storage of food (including Milk Banks), and microorganisms have the ability to adhere to these surfaces (Li et al., 2023; Hayrapetyan et al., 2016; Kim et a., 2017; Abebe 2020; Ling et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2018; Gupta et al., 2018; Iversen et al., 2004; Liu et al., 2022; Kown et al., 2017; McMullan et al. ., 2018; Nyenje et al., 2013; Hurrell et al., 2009; Pestourie et al., 2014; Jassberger et al., 2020). - rephrase this

The choice of surfaces used in testing plays a vital role in the preparation, distribution, and storage of food, including Milk Banks. Microorganisms possess the ability to adhere to these surfaces, as evidenced by studies conducted by Li et al. (2023), Hayrapetyan et al. (2016), Kim et al. (2017), Abebe (2020), Ling et al. (2020), Zeng et al. (2018), Gupta et al. (2018), Iversen et al. (2004), Liu et al. (2022), Kown et al. (2017), McMullan et al. (2018), Nyenje et al. (2013), Hurrell et al. (2009), Pestourie et al. (2014), and Jassberger et al. (2020).
Kumari and Sarkar (2014) also demonstrated the ability of B. cereus to produce biofilm at 4°C, and in a study by Park et al. (2020), B. cereus produced biofilm at 7°C. - rephrase this

Kumari and Sarkar (2014) provided evidence of B. cereus's ability to produce biofilm even at low temperatures of 4°C. Similarly, in a study conducted by Park et al. (2020), B. cereus was observed to form biofilms at a slightly higher temperature of 7°C.

The process of biofilm formation by various microorganisms is complex. For the characterization of microbiological hazards, mathematical prognostic models can be used to describe the formation of biofilm and assess the risk of microbial contamination. In this study, response surface models were developed to show biofilm formation as a function of time and inoculum density. - rephrase this

Biofilm formation by different microorganisms is a multifaceted process. To analyze microbiological risks, mathematical prognostic models can be employed to understand biofilm formation and evaluate the potential for microbial contamination. In this study, response surface models were created to illustrate the relationship between biofilm formation, time, and inoculum density.

Therefore, it can be considered as a useful tool for assessing the influence of various factors on the complex phenomenon of biofilm formation. The risk of biofilm formation is highly dependent on inoculum density and microbial stressors. The density of the inoculum is critical as the number of microorganisms in the biofilm will determine the level of food contamination. - rephrase this

Hence, response surface models can be deemed a valuable tool for evaluating the impact of different factors on the intricate process of biofilm formation. The risk associated with biofilm formation is greatly influenced by both the inoculum density and microbial stressors. The inoculum density plays a crucial role as it determines the number of microorganisms present in the biofilm, directly affecting the level of food contamination.

In the case of PVC pipes, the greatest risk of biofilm formation on its surface was found in the case of Enterobacter cloacae . The developed model predicting biofilm production allows for limited use. A similar situation of limited confidence in the developed model occurs in the case of Cronobacter sakazakii biofilm formation on the surface of stainless steel. Parameter estimates and fit to data are not satisfactory. In the case of the remaining bacteria forming biofilm on the surface of stainless steel, the developed models can be used to assess the risk of biofilm formation in facilities where stainless steel is a working surface. - rephrase this

Among the various microorganisms tested, Enterobacter cloacae posed the highest risk of biofilm formation on the surface of PVC pipes. However, the model developed to predict biofilm production has limited applicability. A similar situation arises with the model developed for Cronobacter sakazakii biofilm formation on stainless steel surfaces, as both the parameter estimates and data fit are unsatisfactory. Nonetheless, for the other bacteria that form biofilms on stainless steel surfaces, the developed models can be utilized to evaluate the risk of biofilm formation in facilities where stainless steel is employed as a working surface.

Most of the developed models are characterized by a high value of the R 2 coefficient and low MSE error values, and they can be used in the assessment of microbiological risk in Breast Milk Banks, neonatal wards and neonatal intensive care units. - rephrase this

The majority of the developed models exhibit high values of the R^2 coefficient and low MSE error values, indicating their effectiveness in assessing microbiological risks in settings such as Breast Milk Banks, neonatal wards, and neonatal intensive care units. These models can be employed to evaluate the potential microbiological risks associated with biofilm formation in these environments.
